_comment: Example: Council of Elders — a social resilience entity. Demonstrates how human governance systems can be represented with scoped evidence.
name: Council of Elders
description: Multi-generational human governance system based on earned trust, long-view deliberation, and distributed authority. Found across unrelated cultures (Haudenosaunee, Māori, Igbo, Tibetan, Aboriginal Australian) — a cross-domain rhyme in human social organization.

scope.definition: Number of future generations explicitly considered in council deliberations. Haudenosaunee (Iroquois) Great Law mandates seven-generation foresight in all decisions affecting land and community.

evidence.source: Haudenosaunee Great Law of Peace (~1450 CE, oral tradition documented 1900s); similar horizon found in Māori tikanga and Aboriginal Australian law.
cross_domain_examples: Tibetan recognition of reincarnated teachers (30+ year horizon)
Welsh court of elders (500+ year land memory)

constraints: oral tradition must be maintained across generations
council must retain authority (not supplanted by centralized state)

scope.falsifiability: If councils consistently make decisions that harm the third or fourth generation without course-correction, the seven-generation model fails. Long-run land health metrics can test this.
scope.measurement_limits: Horizon is symbolic in some traditions; literal in others. Operationalization varies.

scope.definition: Fraction of major council decisions reversed or significantly modified within one generation (25 years), as a proxy for deliberative quality under slower consensus-building.

evidence.source: Comparison of Haudenosaunee treaty adherence vs. colonial treaty reversal rates (Cornell Haudenosaunee studies); Māori Treaty jurisprudence analysis.

condition.environment: council retains governing authority over decision domain
constraints: no external override by colonial/state authority

scope.falsifiability: Reversal rate is meaningless if external authority overrides council decisions — this contaminates the measure.
scope.measurement_limits: Hard to measure pre-colonial; modern examples often operate under state constraints.

attributes.consensus_protocol: Decisions require broad agreement (not majority vote). Objection is a form of teaching, not obstruction. Deliberation continues until all voices are heard — the council does not close until the silence is comfortable.
attributes.legitimacy_source: Earned through demonstrated long-view reasoning, memory of precedent, and willingness to absorb short-term cost for long-term coherence. Not granted by election alone.
attributes.pattern: earned authority, long-view deliberation, distributed memory, ceremonial renewal

links.note: Both demonstrate earned leadership structures based on demonstrated competence and social trust.
Council decisions encode intergenerational memory; echo is the mechanism.
Council is the social analogue of mycelium: distributed, slow-to-update, resilient, memory-bearing.
